## Bits and bytes
By the way, what is 123? 
We know that 123 is 1\*10$^2$+2\*10$^1$+3\*10$^0$. This is **decimal numeral system**, which is used everywhere.
But, in computer science we can\`t do it like these, we need to optimize operations. Here we use special, **binary numeral system**. It ensures reliability, because it have only two conditions: on and off, true and false, or 1 and 0. **And, 1 or 0 is One Bit**. 
**One byte is 8 bit**. For example, if we type 10010111 - it takes **one byte**
And how can we count to, for example, 7? Easy! 00000111 is 7, because each digit place of 00000111 is 2 raised to different powers, beginning with 2$^0$, and finishing with2$^7$, from right to the left. And if number contains zero, we skip digit place, in fact **multiply it by zero.**

| 0       | 0       | 0       | 0       | 0       | 1       | 1       | 1       |
| ------- | ------- | ------- | ------- | ------- | ------- | ------- | ------- |
| $2^7$*0 | $2^6$*0 | $2^5$*0 | $2^4$*0 | $2^3$*0 | $2^2$*1 | $2^1$*1 | $2^0$*1 |

Max value here is 255, and in past computers could only show 256 colors on the screen(0-255 range gives 256 possible values), but with increase of video memory nowadays we are able to see 16,7 millions of colors on our screens.

## ASCII 
Letters, the same way as colors, are encoded with **ASCII**, using **8 bite system**. For example **A** is 01000001, which is 65. This is a capital letter, although we can transform it into lowercase letter. If 01000001 is **A**, than 01100001 is **a**. And we can turn any capital letter into lowercase letter easily with adding **1** into digit place corresponding to **32**. And here, in **ASCII we have only 256 symbols**.
**![686](data:image/png;base64,)**
## Unicode
**ASCII** is enough for English, but we have hundreds of languages all over the world, and because of this humanity invented **Unicode**, which is standard of encoding used **universally**. It is capable of encoding **over 1 000 000 symbols**, but **only 150 000 are being used** so far! **Unicode** is variable-length and uses between 1 and 4 bytes per character, depending on its complexity. First 128 symbols in **Unicode** match with first 128 symbols of **ASCII**. It was done for **backward combability**, between two encoding standards.
## RGB
In RGB color is made from **3 values, or channels**, and each channel is 1 byte - (Red; Green; Blue). So color is being formed from **3 intensities**, it is like a mixture of **3 main colors**. Computers **don\`t store images, they store numbers**.
## Algorithms
Algorithms are one of the most basic things in Computer Science. There is an example: while searching for a name in phone book, the most obvious way is to **turn pages one by one**. Eventually you\`ll find the name, but it would take **big amount of time**, so let\`s find another way! We can turn **two pages at a time**, but there are a **50% chance that we will skip the name!** But if we program the machine to stop when reaching required letter, and to go on turning pages one by one, **we eliminate risk of missing it**, and we do the same job, **but twice faster**. However, there are **one more way** to solve this problem. If we go to the **middle of the book**, we will be on the letter **P**, for example. And if we need **name**, which begins with letter **Y**, we know, in which of the two halves we need to go and **divide** it. I know, sounds weird, but in fact **it is much faster** then both of the previous ways.**![](data:image/png;base64,)**
## Pseudocode
Pseudocode is code, but written with simple words from simple language. 
Here goes an example of such code.
````
1  Pick up phone book
2  Open to middle of phone book
3  Look at page
4  If person is on page
5      Call person
6  Else if person is earlier in book
7      Open to middle of left half of book
8      Go back to line 3
9  Else if person is later in book
10     Open to middle of right half of book
11     Go back to line 3
12 Else
13     Quit
````
This code is used to show basics of coding and logic. It doesn\'t used in real life tasks.
## Scratch
This is a block-based language, made a long time ago at M.I.T., to give people around the world the opportunity to learn how to code, especially school-age kids.
